action.content: Write a casual blog post about how the outer solar system is different from the inner solar system, and what that means for the possibility of life. Wrap your entire response with double quotation marks. Your response should contain 17 or more sentences.
action.label: context

action.content: "Hey there, today I'm going to take you on a journey through our solar system, starting with the inner planets and moving on to the outer planets. The inner planets, Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars, are all rocky and relatively small. They're close to the sun, which means they're hot and dry. The outer planets, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune, are all gas giants and are much larger than the inner planets. They're far from the sun, which means they're cold and icy. The outer planets have a lot of moons, and some of them have a lot of interesting features, like Saturn's rings and Jupiter's Great Red Spot. The outer planets are also much more likely to have life than the inner planets, because they're colder and have more water ice. The inner planets are too hot and dry for life to exist, so we can't expect to find any there. The outer planets, on the other hand, are a much more promising place to look for life. So, that's a quick overview of the inner and outer planets.
action.label: response
